title: Closed-Loop Geothermal: Why Well Intersection Is the Hard Part
lede: Next-generation geothermal has a reputation problem: people assume the challenge is heat. It isn't. The heat is there. The hard part is hitting the target — connecting two wellbores precisely, thousands of feet underground. That is a ranging problem, and it is already solved.
blocks: A closed-loop geothermal system circulates fluid through a sealed underground loop to harvest heat without producing reservoir fluids. To form that loop, two wells must intersect — or be connected — at depth with high precision. Enhanced systems add a further demand: multiple wellbores spaced exactly to access specific zones of a heat reservoir.
At the depths involved, conventional directional surveys are not accurate enough to guarantee that connection. Miss it, and the loop doesn't close — and the project's economics collapse with it.
Heat isn't the bottleneck. Hitting the target is — and magnetic ranging is built for exactly that.
Borrowed from the hardest wells in oil & gas

Intentionally intersecting one wellbore with another is not new. It is the same physics that makes relief wells, SAGD well pairs and coal-bed-methane intersect drilling possible. Magnetic ranging has guided those connections for decades.
blocks2: Gunnar applies active ranging — including the Torped™ rotating-magnet source and DeadSet™ — to drive one wellbore into precise connection with another, and passive ranging to hold exact separation between parallel wells. The result is a deterministic connection where conventional steering offers only probability.
That precision unlocks designs that were previously impractical: tighter loops, multi-lateral collectors, and reservoir spacing tuned to the resource.
A toolset for the geothermal engineer

Gunnar's leadership has detailed this crossover at venues including the Geothermal Resources Council and in coverage by ThinkGeoEnergy, positioning magnetic ranging as a core capability the geothermal drilling engineer can now design around — not a workaround, but a foundation.
takeaways: Closed-loop geothermal depends on connecting wellbores precisely at depth.
Conventional surveys can't guarantee that connection; ranging can.
The physics is proven — it's how relief wells and SAGD pairs are drilled.
Active ranging makes the connection; passive ranging holds precise spacing.
ctaTitle: Designing a closed-loop or EGS project?
ctaBody: Talk to us about engineering the intersection your geothermal design depends on.
